source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
The J-15 carrier-based aircraft of our navy has successfully landed and taken off from the aircraft carrier "Liaoning." The aircraft takeoff model is simplified as follows: the aircraft first undergoes uniformly accelerated linear motion on the horizontal deck, then accelerates with maximum power on the inclined deck at an angle of $\theta=15^{\circ}$, and finally takes off from the deck at a speed of $360\mathrm{km/h}$, as shown in the figure. The mass of the aircraft is 18 tons, the length of deck $AB$ is $180\mathrm{m}$, and the length of $BC$ is $50\mathrm{m}$. (Ignore the length of the aircraft, neglect all friction and air resistance, take $\sin15^{\circ}{=}0.3$, and $g\mathrm{=}10\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^{2}$.) If the speed at point $B$ on the deck is required to be at least $60\%$ of the speed when leaving the inclined deck.

1. What is the minimum traction force required for an aircraft to take off when moving on a horizontal deck?
2. If the aircraft reaches its maximum power exactly at point $B$, how much time is required from the start of the aircraft's motion until it leaves the deck?

issues: No existence/format/size metadata is provided for the image path listed in metadata.image_paths.

image_dependency: question_text explicitly says "as shown in the figure" and uses diagram labels A, B, C plus horizontal/inclined deck geometry
asset_registry_record shows one valid image asset exists and registry_passed=true
The text also states key lengths and angle, so image dependence appears strong but not absolute
multi_step: The motion is split into at least two phases: uniformly accelerated motion on AB and accelerated motion on inclined BC
The problem asks for two outputs: minimum traction force and total time until leaving the deck
Solving requires combining multiple conditions: final takeoff speed, speed-at-B lower-bound of 60% of leaving speed, deck lengths, incline angle, and gravity
This is a quantitative physics setup involving staged kinematics/dynamics rather than a single direct lookup
verifiability: answer_type=short_text with two explicit numeric results
The target answers are quantitative values with units, making rule-based or symbolic checking feasible
Given lengths, angle, mass, gravity, and speed constraints, the solution path is well specified enough for independent verification

risk_flags: No existence/format/size metadata is provided for the image path listed in metadata.image_paths.

decision_reasons: Question, answer, and one valid image asset are all present, so the core semantic content is intact.
Initial scoring is strong: image_dependency_score=0.78, multi_step_score=0.89, verifiability_score=0.93.
The registry passed, and the available assets appear sufficient for recovery and verification.
Recorded issues are metadata inconsistencies about image_paths rather than loss of core problem content.

variants.rewritten_question_text:
The J-15 carrier-based aircraft of our navy has successfully landed and taken off from the aircraft carrier "Liaoning." The aircraft takeoff model is simplified as follows: the aircraft first undergoes uniformly accelerated linear motion on the horizontal deck, then accelerates with maximum power on the inclined deck at an angle of $\theta=15^{\circ}$, and finally takes off from the deck at a speed of $360\mathrm{km/h}$, as shown in the figure. The mass of the aircraft is 18 tons, the length of deck $AB$ is $180\mathrm{m}$, and the length of $BC$ is $50\mathrm{m}$. (Ignore the length of the aircraft, neglect all friction and air resistance, take $\sin15^{\circ}{=}0.3$, and $g\mathrm{=}10\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^{2}$.) If the speed at point $B$ on the deck is required to be at least $60\%$ of the speed when leaving the inclined deck.

1. What is the minimum traction force required for an aircraft to take off when moving on a horizontal deck?
2. If the aircraft reaches its maximum power exactly at point $B$, how much time is required from the start of the aircraft's motion until it leaves the deck?

open_ended_problem_variants.title: PhysReason 开放题
